title: AI Agent Readiness Audit: 18 Questions That Tell You Which Workflows to Automate, Augment, or Leave Alone
subtitle: Built for operations leaders at 10-200 person professional services firms deciding between hiring staff or deploying agents. Score your firm in 12 minutes, get a tier, a category breakdown, and a specific next move per workflow category.

answers.label: Boutique (1-10 people) — founder still in most delivery
Growing (11-30 people) — first ops hire made or imminent
Established (31-100 people) — multiple service lines, dedicated ops function
Scaling (101-200 people) — partner-led, formal delivery leadership

questions.text: How consolidated is the data for your most painful workflow?

answers.label: 1 — Nobody can fully map where it lives
2 — 4+ systems, some in spreadsheets or email
3 — 2-3 systems but handoffs break weekly
4 — 2-3 connected systems with clean handoffs
5 — Single source of truth in one system

questions.text: How structured is the intake data when a new client, case, or project enters your pipeline?

answers.label: 1 — Mostly free-form email or phone notes
2 — Under 30% structured, the rest is narrative
3 — Roughly 50/50 structured vs. free text
4 — 60-80% structured fields
5 — 80%+ structured (dropdowns, dates, numbers)

questions.text: How consistently do the same fields get filled the same way across team members?

answers.label: 1 — Everyone has their own format and abbreviations
2 — Some conventions exist but drift constantly
3 — Documented but enforced only on new hires
4 — Standardized with occasional drift on edge cases
5 — Validated at entry, enforced across the team

questions.text: How quickly can you pull the last 12 months of a specific workflow's data into one clean table?

answers.label: 1 — Would require a multi-week manual project
2 — Possible but takes days of cleanup
3 — A few hours of export and reconciling
4 — Under an hour with existing reports
5 — One query or dashboard, already live

low.headline: Your data isn't ready — don't buy an agent yet
low.text: Agents read whatever you feed them, and right now you'd be feeding them fog. Before any AI workflow ships, you need one painful workflow where intake is structured and the source of truth is obvious. Otherwise every agent output is a coin flip.
steps: Pick the single workflow with the highest pain and map every system its data touches
Replace free-text intake with structured forms (dropdowns, dates, enumerated fields) — Typeform, Fillout, or a branded form builder
Consolidate to one system of record for that workflow before anything else

mid.headline: Good bones, but handoffs are leaking
mid.text: You have structure where it counts but the seams between systems are where agents will fail silently. The next gain is closing those seams, not adding intelligence on top of broken plumbing.
steps: Audit every system-to-system handoff in your top 3 workflows and log where data is re-keyed
Enforce field validation at the entry point so bad data can't enter the pipeline
Build one consolidated view (warehouse, Airtable, or Supabase) that unifies the 2-3 systems before the agent layer

high.headline: Your data layer is agent-ready
high.text: Keep sharpening — here's the frontier. At this level the bottleneck shifts from data hygiene to data enrichment: layering context that humans carry in their heads into the same structured store an agent reads.
steps: Capture the soft signals (priority reasoning, risk flags, client tone) as structured fields, not free-text
Version your schemas so agents don't break when you add fields
Move toward an event-sourced log so agents can replay decisions and you can audit them

questions.text: How documented are the decision rules for your most repeatable workflow?

answers.label: 1 — All in people's heads
2 — Informal notes in Slack or Notion
3 — Documented but stale
4 — Documented and updated quarterly
5 — Documented, versioned, and referenced daily

questions.text: How often does the workflow hit an edge case that breaks the standard path?

answers.label: 1 — Every other case is an exception
2 — A quarter of cases need judgment calls
3 — Roughly 10-15% need escalation
4 — Under 10%, predictable categories
5 — Under 5%, all edge cases are enumerated

questions.text: How transferable is the senior judgment on this workflow to a new hire?

answers.label: 1 — Takes a year and still inconsistent
2 — 6+ months of shadowing
3 — 2-3 months with heavy review
4 — A few weeks with a clear checklist
5 — Under a week because the logic is written down

low.headline: You're automating tribal knowledge — that's a trap
low.text: If your senior people can't explain the rules, an agent won't learn them either. The first move isn't AI, it's documentation. Every firm that tried to skip this step ended up with agents that hallucinated the decisions their best people make by instinct.
steps: Shadow your senior operator on the workflow for a week and capture every decision point
Turn those decisions into an if-then rulebook, not a narrative SOP
Run the rulebook past a junior hire — if they can't execute it, it's not ready for an agent

mid.headline: Logic is partially legible — tighten the edges
mid.text: The happy path is understood, but your agents will fail on the 15% of edge cases nobody wrote down. Agent-ready logic means the exceptions are enumerated, not absorbed by a senior brain.
steps: Audit the last 90 days of escalations and categorize every edge case
Write an explicit handoff rule for each category (agent handles or human handles)
Add a confidence threshold so the agent escalates on anything outside the enumerated set

high.headline: Decision logic is agent-grade
high.text: Keep sharpening — here's the frontier. When your rulebook is this clean, the gain moves to feedback loops: letting the agent's outputs sharpen the rules over time without silent drift.
steps: Instrument every agent decision with a human review sample rate (start at 10%)
Capture overrides as training signal, not just corrections
Review rule changes monthly — treat your decision logic like a product, not a document

questions.text: How narrow is the scope of the workflow you'd automate first?

answers.label: 1 — End-to-end delivery, dozens of steps
2 — A full service line with branching paths
3 — A clear phase with some variability
4 — A single sub-process with defined inputs and outputs
5 — One task, one input type, one output type

questions.text: How high is the volume of this workflow per month?

answers.label: 1 — Under 10 times per month
2 — 10-30 per month
3 — 30-100 per month
4 — 100-500 per month
5 — 500+ per month or continuous

questions.text: How clearly can you state the exact inputs and outputs of the workflow on a single page?

answers.label: 1 — Can't — it depends too much on context
2 — Rough sketch, lots of asterisks
3 — Mostly clear but a few fuzzy edges
4 — One page, minor ambiguity
5 — One page, zero ambiguity, already written down

questions.text: How measurable is the success of a single run of this workflow?

answers.label: 1 — We'd know a month later, if at all
2 — Measurable but nobody checks
3 — Measured quarterly
4 — Measured weekly with clear KPIs
5 — Measured per run with automated scoring

low.headline: Your scope is too wide to automate
low.text: Agent-ready workflows are narrow by design. If you're trying to automate 'client onboarding' or 'proposal delivery' as a whole, you'll fail. The Agent-Ready Ops methodology starts with one task, one input type, one output type — everything else is scope creep dressed up as ambition.
steps: Break your target workflow into its smallest repeatable unit with a single input and output
Pick the unit that happens most often (volume is the multiplier on ROI)
Write the inputs, outputs, and success criteria on one page before any build starts

mid.headline: Scope is tightening — now verify volume and measurability
mid.text: You've narrowed the target but the economics still need to pencil. An automation that runs 10 times a month won't pay back; one that runs 500 times will. Make sure the narrow workflow you picked actually has the frequency to justify it.
steps: Confirm monthly volume of the target workflow is above 100 runs — otherwise pick a higher-volume neighbor
Define a per-run success metric and start measuring it manually for a month
Lock the scope in writing so no stakeholder expands it mid-build

high.headline: Scope discipline is tight
high.text: Keep sharpening — here's the frontier. At this level the risk isn't scope creep, it's scope atrophy: an agent that keeps running on a workflow that no longer matters. Build the review cadence now.
steps: Quarterly, re-validate that each deployed agent still owns a workflow with live volume and live value
Sunset agents whose workflow volume drops below your threshold
Keep a staged pipeline of the next 3 narrow workflows so you never run out of build targets

questions.text: How deliberately is a human review step designed into the workflows you'd automate?

answers.label: 1 — No review layer planned
2 — Ad-hoc spot checks only
3 — Review exists but nobody owns it
4 — Owned review with clear criteria
5 — Structured review with sampling, overrides, and feedback capture

questions.text: How clear is the override path when the agent gets it wrong?

answers.label: 1 — No override path defined
2 — Somebody emails somebody
3 — Logged but not triaged
4 — Routed to a named owner with SLA
5 — Automatic escalation with learning capture

questions.text: How calibrated is your team's trust in the agent's output?

answers.label: 1 — Team either rubber-stamps or ignores it
2 — Trust varies wildly by person
3 — General sense of when to trust, undocumented
4 — Confidence thresholds set per output type
5 — Confidence thresholds enforced with automated gating

low.headline: Without review layers, agents compound errors
low.text: Deploying an agent without a human-in-the-loop design is how firms end up with confidently-wrong outputs going to clients. The review layer isn't overhead — it's the thing that turns an experiment into a production system.
steps: Design the review step before the agent — name the reviewer, the criteria, and the sample rate
Build an override path that's one click, not an email chain
Log every override as structured training signal, not a comment thread

mid.headline: Review exists but ownership is fuzzy
mid.text: The review layer only works if one person owns it with a clear SLA. Shared ownership is no ownership — and that's where agent drift starts.
steps: Assign a named owner per deployed agent with weekly review responsibility
Set confidence thresholds per output type and route low-confidence outputs to human first
Hold a monthly override review to turn corrections into rule updates

high.headline: Your review architecture is production-grade
high.text: Keep sharpening — here's the frontier. With calibrated trust and clean overrides, the next gain is reducing human load without losing oversight — sample-based review, not 100% review.
steps: Move from full review to statistical sampling once an agent clears a 90-day accuracy benchmark
Build a real-time dashboard of agent confidence, override rate, and drift
Use override patterns to predict where the next agent should be deployed

questions.text: How clearly do you know the cost of hiring the next person vs. building a system to replace that hire?

answers.label: 1 — Never compared them side by side
2 — Rough gut sense, no numbers
3 — Some numbers but incomplete
4 — Modeled for the top 2 roles
5 — Modeled across every role, reviewed quarterly

questions.text: How disciplined are you about demanding a 90-day payback on any automation investment?

answers.label: 1 — No payback standard exists
2 — We talk about ROI but don't measure
3 — Measured post-hoc, inconsistently
4 — Required on paper, sometimes enforced
5 — Enforced per project with named owner

questions.text: How visibly do you track which workflows consume the most senior-hour capacity?

answers.label: 1 — No visibility on where senior hours go
2 — Anecdotal sense from partner meetings
3 — Tracked but rarely reviewed
4 — Monthly capacity review
5 — Live dashboard of senior-hour drain by workflow

questions.text: How much of your next 12 months of growth can you absorb without adding headcount?

answers.label: 1 — Every new client means a new hire
2 — Maybe 10-20% more volume before hiring
3 — Up to 30% before the next hire
4 — 30-60% growth absorbed by existing systems
5 — Can scale without scaling payroll

low.headline: You're hiring when you should be systematizing
low.text: Every firm that scales profitably hits a point where the next hire is actually a system, not a person. If you're not modeling hire-vs-build, you're leaving the 90-Day Payback Rule on the table — and paying payroll for work a well-scoped agent could do for a fraction.
steps: Model the fully-loaded cost of your next hire vs. a one-time build for the same workflow
Instrument senior-hour drain so you can see where the payroll is actually going
Pick the highest-drain workflow and run the Hire a System decision on it this quarter

mid.headline: Economics are visible, discipline is patchy
mid.text: You see the numbers but enforcement is inconsistent. The 90-Day Payback Rule only works when it's a hard gate, not a soft preference — otherwise you accumulate half-built automations with no owner and no payback.
steps: Require a written payback model before any automation spend over $5,000
Name an owner per deployed system accountable for the payback number
Kill or rebuild any automation that misses its 90-day mark

high.headline: You're running the Hire a System playbook
high.text: Keep sharpening — here's the frontier. At this level the compounding play is turning your systems into operating leverage that grows faster than headcount — the real definition of scaling without scaling payroll.
steps: Shift from payback-per-project to portfolio-level capacity freed per quarter
Build a reinvestment loop: hours saved fund the next system, not discretionary spend
Benchmark revenue-per-employee quarterly and treat it as the north-star efficiency metric

ctas.headline: Want a working call on your agent-readiness gaps?
ctas.description: Book a 30-minute working call where I'll walk you through your exact answers, stack them against professional services firms your size, and pinpoint the ONE workflow I'd automate first. No pitch — if we both see signal, we keep going.

ctas.headline: Under 10 people? You're early for a custom audit.
ctas.description: Automation ROI at your scale is real but payback is longer and the Hire a System math doesn't pencil until you've got repeat workflows at volume. Follow me on LinkedIn — I post tactical professional services automation breakdowns 3x/week. When you cross 10-15 people, come back and retake; the CTA will change.

ctas.headline: You're running tight — here's what professional services firms your size miss next.
ctas.description: At your score, the obvious leaks are closed. The next gains come from event-sourced decision logs that let agents learn from overrides, confidence-gated sampling that replaces 100% review, and portfolio-level capacity accounting that turns hours saved into compounding leverage. I'm writing teardowns on each — follow along.

ctas.headline: Get the weekly professional services ops teardown
ctas.description: One tactical case study + one n8n workflow breakdown per week. How mid-market professional services firms scale without scaling payroll — no fluff, no pitch.
